metadata.title: Portfolio Analysis: Steal Like a Pro
metadata.playlist: Name It & Brand It — Your Strategic Identity
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 6 brief — Brand Strategy + Naming

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 6 of 7. Part 3 of the assignment is worth twenty points, and the fastest way to lose those points is to write vague praise about five portfolios you skimmed. This episode teaches you how to analyze five to eight portfolios specifically — with screenshots, with named elements, and with the one thing you would improve. The synthesis is the second deliverable — it pulls the patterns across all of them into a set of best practices you can actually apply. Let's go.

props.segment: Portfolio Analysis: Steal Like a Pro
props.command: 5–8 portfolios. Per portfolio: 5 dimensions + admired element + improvement. Then best-practices synthesis.
props.runningText: specific beats vague — named examples and screenshots, not vibes…
output: 5–8 portfolio analyses with screenshots and specific observations
Best-practices synthesis: patterns across all portfolios

beats.act: what to look for — five dimensions
beats.narration_text: For each portfolio, analyze five dimensions. First: structure — how is the portfolio organized? Does it have a clear hierarchy? Can you find a specific project in under thirty seconds? Second: project showcasing — how does it present individual projects? Does each project explain the problem, the process, and the result? Or does it show a screenshot with no context? Third: storytelling — does the portfolio have a narrative arc? Does it explain why this person built these things in this order, or does it read as a list? Fourth: technical demonstration — how does it prove competence? Code samples, live demos, documented outputs, or just assertions? Fifth: standout elements — what is the one thing that makes this portfolio memorable in a way the others are not? It might be the visual design, an unusual project, a clarity of voice, or a specific proof of competence that most portfolios in the field leave out. Document all five for each portfolio. The goal is to have a specific observation for each dimension — not 'well structured' but 'the nav sorts projects by problem type rather than technology, which makes it easier to read as a hiring manager looking for domain fit.'

artifactLines: Structure: clear hierarchy? Can you find a project in under 30 seconds?
Project showcasing: problem + process + result per project, or just screenshots?
Storytelling: narrative arc — why these projects in this order?
Technical demonstration: code samples, live demos, documented outputs, or assertions?
Standout: the one thing memorable that others in the field leave out.
props.sparkLine: Specific observation per dimension — not 'well structured.' That's not analysis.

beats.narration_text: For each of the five to eight portfolios, write four things. One: what you admire — with a screenshot. Name the element, describe what it does well, and embed a screenshot of that specific element or page. The screenshot is not decoration — it is the evidence that you actually looked at the portfolio and can point to something specific. Two: why it is effective — one to two sentences explaining the mechanism. Not 'it looks good' — 'the case study structure leads with the business problem rather than the technical solution, which means a non-technical hiring manager can assess it without reading the code.' Three: how it could inspire yours — one sentence connecting this element to your own work. Not 'I should do this too' — 'my tool's strongest proof point is the documented audit trail, and this portfolio structure would let me lead with that outcome rather than the architecture.' Four: one thing you would improve. This is the most important part. A person who can articulate an improvement to a good portfolio is a person who has thought about what good looks like. 'The mobile layout breaks on the case study pages and makes the code samples unreadable' is a real observation. 'I might change the colors' is not.

artifactLines: 1. What you admire + screenshot: name the element, describe it, embed the screenshot.
2. Why it's effective: mechanism (1–2 sentences) — not 'it looks good.'
3. How it inspires yours: connect to your own strongest proof point (1 sentence).
4. One thing to improve: specific and actionable — not 'I might change the colors.'
Screenshot = evidence. No screenshot = you described a portfolio you didn't look at.
props.sparkLine: 4 items. Screenshot is evidence. Improvement is where you prove you understand quality.

beats.narration_text: After you have analyzed five to eight portfolios individually, write the synthesis. The synthesis is not a summary of each portfolio — it is the patterns across all of them. What do the best portfolios in your target field do consistently? What do the weakest portfolios in the group consistently fail at? What is the one thing that separates a portfolio a hiring manager would remember from one they would not? Write three to five best practices, each as a specific, actionable statement — not 'have good projects' but 'lead each case study with the business problem and the outcome before the technical method, because most readers decide whether to go deeper within the first two sentences.' Then the personal application: which of these best practices can you implement immediately, which require a project you do not yet have, and which require tools or skills you are building in this course. That last question connects the portfolio analysis to the work you are doing — this is not just a research task, it is a design brief for your own portfolio.

props.artifactHeading: Patterns across all portfolios — not a summary of each
artifactLines: 3–5 best practices: specific + actionable. Not 'have good projects' — name the mechanism.
Patterns to find: what the best ones do consistently; what the weakest consistently fail at.
What separates a memorable portfolio from one a hiring manager forgets?
Personal application: implement now / need a project / need a skill you're building here.
This analysis is a design brief for your own portfolio — say so explicitly.
props.sparkLine: Patterns across all. Then your design brief. Not 5 individual summaries.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Portfolio Analysis. Five to eight portfolios. For each: five dimensions — structure, showcasing, storytelling, technical demonstration, standout — with specific observations per dimension. A screenshot of the element you admire. A one-sentence explanation of the mechanism. A one-sentence connection to your own work. One specific improvement you would make. No vague praise. No 'I might change the colors.' Then the synthesis: three to five best practices drawn from the patterns across all portfolios, each specific and actionable, plus a personal application that names what you can implement now versus what you are building toward. Vague analysis costs Part 3 points. Specific observations plus screenshots are the standard. Episode 7 is the Madison plus-one and the final submission.

props.artifactTitle: Portfolio Analysis: Steal Like a Pro
artifactLines: 5–8 portfolios. Per portfolio: 5 dimensions (specific) + screenshot + mechanism + connection + improvement.
No vague praise. Screenshot = evidence you actually looked.
Improvement = the most important item. 'Change the colors' is not an improvement.
Synthesis: 3–5 best practices (patterns across all) + personal application.
Analysis is a design brief for your own portfolio. Say so.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the prompt that drafts the analysis for one portfolio. Run it once per portfolio — five to eight times total. Paste this into Claude and fill in the brackets. The prompt reads: I am analyzing a portfolio for Part 3 of INFO 7375 Assignment 6. The portfolio belongs to [name or handle] and is in [field/industry]. I have reviewed it across five dimensions. Here are my observations: Structure: [your notes on organization and navigability]. Project showcasing: [your notes on how individual projects are presented]. Storytelling: [your notes on narrative arc and sequencing]. Technical demonstration: [your notes on proof of competence — code, demos, outputs, assertions]. Standout element: [what makes it memorable]. The thing I would improve: [your specific observation]. Draft a per-portfolio analysis section with these four subsections: what I admire and why it is effective — combine these into two to three sentences with the mechanism, not just praise; how it could inspire my own portfolio — one sentence connecting it to the strongest proof point in my Madison tool work; and one specific, actionable improvement that would make this portfolio stronger. After you have all five to eight drafts, use the synthesis prompt from this episode to identify the patterns across all of them.

props.segment: Portfolio Analysis: Steal Like a Pro
props.command: Portfolio: [name/handle] in [field]. My notes: structure · showcasing · storytelling · technical · standout · one improvement. Draft 4 subsections: admire + mechanism (2–3 sentences) · how it inspires mine (1 sentence) · specific improvement.
props.runningText: run this prompt once per portfolio — 5 to 8 times total; then run the synthesis prompt across all drafts…

beats.narration_text: Portfolio Analysis: Steal Like a Pro. Liam, in for Bear.
